64	# Blueprint Writer

90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>

108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."

131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.

135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. 205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the

192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module

194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product

197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This

211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}

428	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
429	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
430	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
431	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
432	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
433	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
434	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
435	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
436	  parallel pair
437	  \[
438	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
439	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
440	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
441	  \]
442	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
443	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
444	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
445	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
446	\end{lemma}
447	
448	\begin{proof}
449	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
450	  Over each open \(U\) the module
451	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the very
452	  definition of the relative tensor product, the quotient of the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
453	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) by the subgroup generated by
454	  the elements \((r \cdot p) \otimes q - p \otimes (r \cdot q)\); equivalently it is
455	  the coequalizer of the two \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear action maps displayed above. Since
456	  colimits of abelian-group presheaves are computed objectwise, these objectwise
457	  coequalizers assemble into a coequalizer in
458	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\); naturality in \(U\) is the
459	  compatibility of the action maps with the restriction maps of
460	  \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\).
461	\end{proof}

463	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
464	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
465	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
466	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
467	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
468	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
469	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
470	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
471	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
472	  the sheafification adjunction
473	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
474	  presheaves of modules, and write
475	  \[
476	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
477	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
478	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
479	  \]
480	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
481	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
482	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
483	  \[
484	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
485	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
486	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
487	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
488	  \]
489	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
490	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
491	\end{lemma}
492	
493	\begin{proof}
494	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
495	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
496	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
497	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
498	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
499	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
500	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
501	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
502	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
503	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
504	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
505	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
506	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
507	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
508	
509	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
510	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
511	  is the objectwise relative tensor
512	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
513	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
514	  maps
515	  \[
516	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
517	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
518	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
519	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
520	  \]
521	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
522	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
523	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
524	  pair
525	  \[
526	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
527	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
528	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
529	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
530	  \]
531	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
532	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
533	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
534	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
535	
536	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
537	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
538	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
539	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
540	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
541	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
542	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
543	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
544	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
545	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
546	  comparison maps
547	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
548	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
549	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
550	  isomorphisms.
551	
552	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
553	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
554	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
555	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
556	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
557	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
558	\end{proof}

560	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
561	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
563	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
564	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
565	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
566	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
567	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
568	  \[
569	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
570	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
571	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
572	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
573	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
574	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
575	  \]
576	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
577	  arguments.
578	\end{corollary}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
582	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
583	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
584	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
585	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
586	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
587	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
588	  \[
589	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
590	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
591	    \qquad
592	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
593	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
594	  \]
595	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
596	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
597	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
598	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
599	  \[
600	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
601	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
602	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
603	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
604	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
605	  \]
606	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
607	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
608	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
609	  morphism
610	  \[
611	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
612	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
613	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
614	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
615	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
616	  \]
617	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
618	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
619	  \[
620	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
621	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
622	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
623	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
624	  \]
625	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
626	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
627	  composite
628	  \[
629	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
630	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
631	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
632	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
633	  \]
634	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
635	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
636	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
637	\end{proof}

639	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
640	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
642	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
643	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
644	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
645	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
646	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
647	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
648	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
649	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
650	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
651	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
652	  %  (formulation omitted)."
653	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
654	  power isomorphisms).}
655	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
656	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
657	  \[
658	    \mu_{m,m'} :
659	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
660	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
661	  \]
662	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
663	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
664	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
665	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
666	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
667	\end{lemma}
668	
669	\begin{proof}
670	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
671	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
672	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
673	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
674	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
675	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
676	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
677	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
678	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
679	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
680	
681	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
682	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
683	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
684	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
685	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
686	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
687	  sheaf tensor products
688	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
689	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
690	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
691	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
692	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
693	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
694	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
695	  of the tensor product is never used.
696	
697	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
698	  \(m = 0\),
699	  \[
700	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
701	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
702	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
703	  \]
704	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
705	  recursive definition
706	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
707	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
708	  \[
709	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
710	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
711	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
712	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
713	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
714	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
715	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
716	  \]
717	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
718	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
719	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
720	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
721	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
722	  \[
723	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
724	      \mathcal{L}
725	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
726	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
727	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
728	  \]
729	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
730	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
731	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
732	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
733	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
734	
735	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
736	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
737	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
738	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
739	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
740	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
741	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
742	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
743	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
744	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
745	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
746	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
747	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
748	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
749	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
750	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
751	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
752	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
753	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
754	\end{proof}

756	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
757	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
758	
759	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
760	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
761	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
762	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
763	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
764	global sections.
765	
766	\begin{definition}\leanok
767	[Section multiplication]
768	  \label{def:sectionMul}
769	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
770	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
771	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
772	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
773	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
774	  \[
775	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
776	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
777	    \;\longrightarrow\;
778	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
779	  \]
780	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
781	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
782	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
783	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
784	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
785	  the sheafification unit
786	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
787	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
788	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
789	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
790	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
791	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
792	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
793	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
794	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
795	\end{definition}
796	
797	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
798	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
799	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
800	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
801	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
802	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
803	  the two composites
804	  \[
805	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
806	    \longrightarrow
807	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
808	    \cong
809	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
810	  \]
811	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
812	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
813	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
814	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
815	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
816	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
817	\end{lemma}
818	
819	\begin{proof}
820	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
821	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
822	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
823	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
824	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
825	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
826	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
827	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
828	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
829	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
830	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
831	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
832	  section multiplications.
833	\end{proof}

835	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
836	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
837	
838	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
839	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
840	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
841	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
842	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
843	
844	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
845	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
846	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
847	  \mathlibok
848	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
849	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
850	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
851	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
852	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
853	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
854	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
855	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
856	  \(1 \in A_0\).
857	\end{lemma}
858	
859	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
860	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
861	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
862	  \mathlibok
863	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
864	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
865	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
866	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
867	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
868	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
869	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
870	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
871	  extension of the graded action.
872	\end{lemma}
873	
874	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
875	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
877	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
878	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
879	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
880	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
881	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
882	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
883	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
884	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
885	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
886	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
887	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
888	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
889	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
890	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
891	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
892	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
893	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
894	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
895	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
896	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
897	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
898	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
899	  multiplication is the composite
900	  \[
901	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
902	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
903	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
904	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
905	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
906	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
907	  \]
908	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
909	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
910	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
911	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
912	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
913	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
914	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
915	\end{lemma}
916	
917	\begin{proof}
918	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
919	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
920	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
921	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
922	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
923	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
924	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
925	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
926	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
927	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
928	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
929	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
930	  commutative-semiring structure.
931	\end{proof}

933	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
934	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
935	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}

940	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
941	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).

951	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
952	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
953	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
954	  \(X_s\). The family
955	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
956	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
957	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
958	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
959	  action

962	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
963	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
964	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
965	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
966	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
967	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
968	  \]

970	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using

12	# Section graded ring infrastructure, Layer 1: tensor powers of a sheaf of modules
13	
14	This file builds the Mathlib-absent infrastructure of
15	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_SectionGradedRing.tex`, Layer 1
16	(`sec:sgr_tensor_powers`): the tensor product, tensor powers, and twists of
17	sheaves of modules over a scheme `X`, together with the unitor and braiding
18	isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product.
19	
20	The category `X.Modules = SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf` of sheaves of modules
21	over a scheme carries **no** monoidal structure in Mathlib (the structure sheaf
22	varies the base ring over opens).  Mathlib *does* supply:
23	
24	* the symmetric monoidal structure on the category of **presheaves** of modules
25	  `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`
26	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal`), and
27	* the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification`
28	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification`).
29	
30	We therefore build the tensor product of sheaves of modules as the sheafification
31	of the objectwise (presheaf) tensor product, following
32	[Stacks, Tag 01CA].
33	
34	## Main definitions
35	
36	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification` — the scheme-level
37	  sheafification functor `X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules`.
38	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (`def:sheafTensorObj`) —
39	  `F ⊗ G := (F.toPresheaf ⊗ G.toPresheaf)^#`.
40	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow` (`def:sheafTensorPow`) —
41	  the `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules.
42	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`) —
43	  the `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}`.
44	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso` — the reflective
45	  counit iso `(F.toPresheaf)^# ≅ F`.
46	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso`,
47	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor`,
48	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding` — the left/right unitor and
49	  braiding isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product.
50	
51	The comparison isomorphism `L^{⊗m} ⊗ L^{⊗m'} ≅ L^{⊗(m+m')}`
52	(`lem:sheafTensorPow_add`) is **deferred**: see the handoff note before the end
53	of the file for the single missing ingredient (strong-monoidality of the module
54	sheafification functor) and the launching pad assembled here.
55	-/
56	
57	universe u
58	
59	open CategoryTheory MonoidalCategory Limits
60	
61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
62	
63	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
64	
65	/-- The scheme-level sheafification functor, sending a presheaf of modules over a
66	scheme `X` to its associated sheaf of modules `X.Modules`.  It is the
67	`PresheafOfModules.sheafification` functor for the identity morphism of the
68	underlying presheaf of rings (which is locally bijective).  Non-private because it
69	appears in the statement of `isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
70	noncomputable def sheafification : X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules :=
71	  PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
72	
73	/-- The category `X.PresheafOfModules` of presheaves of modules over a scheme,
74	presented in the exact form `PresheafOfModules (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)`
75	for which Mathlib equips it with a symmetric monoidal structure.  This is
76	*definitionally* `X.PresheafOfModules` (since
77	`X.ringCatSheaf.obj = X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat`), so a term of
78	either type is accepted for the other. -/
79	private abbrev MonoidalPresheaf (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ :=
80	  _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
81	
82	/-- The tensor product of two sheaves of modules over a scheme, defined as the
83	sheafification of the objectwise tensor product presheaf
84	(Mathlib `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  See [Stacks, Tag 01CA]
85	(`def:sheafTensorObj`). -/
86	noncomputable def tensorObj (F G : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
87	  sheafification.obj
88	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
89	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
90	
91	/-- The structure sheaf as a sheaf of modules over itself: the unit object of the
92	tensor product, i.e. the zeroth tensor power `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X`
93	(`lem:moduleUnit_mathlib`). -/
94	private noncomputable abbrev unitModule (X : Scheme.{u}) : X.Modules :=
95	  SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf
96	
97	/-- The `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules over a scheme, defined
98	by recursion: `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X` (the unit module) and
99	`L^{⊗(m+1)} = L^{⊗m} ⊗ L`.  See [Stacks, Tag 01CU] (`def:sheafTensorPow`). -/
100	noncomputable def tensorPow (L : X.Modules) : ℕ → X.Modules
101	  | 0 => unitModule X
102	  | (m + 1) => tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L
103	
104	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_zero (L : X.Modules) : tensorPow L 0 = unitModule X := rfl
105	
106	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_succ (L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) :
107	    tensorPow L (m + 1) = tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L := rfl
108	
109	/-- The `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules `F` by the `m`-th
110	tensor power of a line bundle `L` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`).  This is the
111	degree-`m` carrier of the section graded module. -/
112	noncomputable def moduleTensorPow (F L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) : X.Modules :=
113	  tensorObj F (tensorPow L m)
114	
115	@[simp] private lemma moduleTensorPow_zero (F L : X.Modules) :
116	    moduleTensorPow F L 0 = tensorObj F (unitModule X) := rfl
117	
118	/-! ### Unitor and braiding isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product
119	
120	These are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal structure on `X.Modules`
121	that descend through sheafification from `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`
122	using only *functoriality* of `sheafification` (and, for the unitors, the
123	reflective counit iso) — no strong-monoidality of `sheafification` is needed, so
124	they are axiom-clean.  They are the launching pad for `tensorPowAdd`. -/
125	
126	/-- The counit isomorphism of the module sheafification adjunction: sheafifying
127	the underlying presheaf of a sheaf of modules returns the sheaf itself.  This is
128	an isomorphism because the counit of `sheafification ⊣ toPresheafOfModules` is
129	invertible (the right adjoint `SheafOfModules.forget` is fully faithful).  It is
130	the launching pad for the left-unitor base case of `tensorPowAdd`. -/
131	private noncomputable def sheafificationCounitIso (G : X.Modules) :
132	    sheafification.obj ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G) ≅ G :=
133	  (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction
134	    (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).counit).app G
135	
136	/-- The left-unitor isomorphism `unitModule X ⊗ G ≅ G` of the sheaf tensor
137	product: the presheaf left unitor `λ_` descended through sheafification, composed
138	with the counit iso `sheafificationCounitIso`.  This is the base case (`m = 0`) of
139	`tensorPowAdd`.  Axiom-clean. -/
140	private noncomputable def tensorObjUnitIso (G : X.Modules) :
141	    tensorObj (unitModule X) G ≅ G :=
142	  sheafification.mapIso
143	      (MonoidalCategory.leftUnitor (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
144	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)) ≪≫
145	    sheafificationCounitIso G
146	
147	/-- The right-unitor isomorphism `G ⊗ unitModule X ≅ G` of the sheaf tensor
148	product: the presheaf right unitor `ρ_` descended through sheafification, composed
149	with the counit iso `sheafificationCounitIso`.  Axiom-clean (no monoidal structure
150	on `X.Modules` is required). -/
151	private noncomputable def tensorObjRightUnitor (G : X.Modules) :
152	    tensorObj G (unitModule X) ≅ G :=
153	  sheafification.mapIso
154	      (MonoidalCategory.rightUnitor (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
155	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)) ≪≫
156	    sheafificationCounitIso G
157	
158	/-- The braiding isomorphism `F ⊗ G ≅ G ⊗ F` of the sheaf tensor product,
159	descended through sheafification from the symmetric braiding on
160	`X.PresheafOfModules` (`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  Axiom-clean: the
161	braiding is pure sheafification-functoriality of the presheaf-level braiding, so
162	no monoidal structure on `X.Modules` is required.  This is the symmetry used in
163	the inductive step of `tensorPowAdd`. -/
164	private noncomputable def tensorBraiding (F G : X.Modules) :
165	    tensorObj F G ≅ tensorObj G F :=
166	  sheafification.mapIso
167	    (BraidedCategory.braiding (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
168	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
169	
170	/-! ### Lax-monoidal global sections: the section multiplication
171	
172	The global-sections functor `Γ(X, -)` is only *lax* monoidal: a pair of global
173	sections does not commute with sheafification, so the multiplication is a map,
174	not an isomorphism.  It is nonetheless `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)`-linear and is the data the
175	section graded ring is built from. -/
176	
177	/-- The **section multiplication** (`def:sectionMul`), the `Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-bilinear map
178	`Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,𝒪_X)} Γ(X,G) → Γ(X, F ⊗ G)`.
179	
180	Its domain `(F.toPresheaf ⊗ G.toPresheaf)(X)` is, by the objectwise formula of
181	`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, the `Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-module
182	`Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,𝒪_X)} Γ(X,G)` of elementary tensors of global sections; a pair
183	`(σ, τ)` is sent to `σ ⊗ τ`.  Postcomposing with the global-sections component of
184	the sheafification unit `η : P → P^#` (`def:sheafTensorObj`) lands in
185	`Γ(X, F ⊗ G)`.  As a morphism in `ModuleCat (Γ(X,𝒪_X))` it is automatically
186	`Γ(X,𝒪_X)`-bilinear; this records that linearity.  Axiom-clean: it is pure
187	sheafification-unit naturality, requiring no monoidal structure on `X.Modules`. -/
188	noncomputable def sectionsMul (F G : X.Modules) :
189	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
190	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G)).obj (Opposite.op ⊤) ⟶
191	      (tensorObj F G).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤) :=
192	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app
193	      (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
194	        ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))).app (Opposite.op ⊤)
195	
196	/-! ### The strong-monoidality comparison `isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`
197	
198	Following `analogies/snap-route.md` (Analogue 1) and the blueprint proof of
199	`lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`: module sheafification is the
200	localization functor at the class `W := J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)` of
201	morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying abelian-presheaf morphism is a
202	local isomorphism (a `J.W` for the opens topology `J` on `X`). -/
203	
204	/-- The Grothendieck topology on the opens of the scheme `X`. -/
205	private abbrev opensTopology (X : Scheme.{u}) : GrothendieckTopology (TopologicalSpace.Opens X) :=
206	  Opens.grothendieckTopology (X : TopCat)
207	
208	open MorphismProperty in
209	/-- **Localization criterion for module sheafification.**  The scheme-level module
210	sheafification `sheafification.map f` is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules iff the
211	underlying abelian-presheaf morphism `(PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map f` lies in
212	the weak-equivalence class `J.W` of the opens topology (i.e. is a local isomorphism of
213	abelian-group presheaves).  This is the reduction step of
214	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`: it turns the strong-monoidality comparison
215	into a purely abelian local-isomorphism statement.  Project-local: it specialises
216	`_root_.PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms` to the
217	identity morphism of `X.ringCatSheaf.obj`. -/
218	lemma isIso_sheafification_map_iff {P Q : X.PresheafOfModules} (f : P ⟶ Q) :
219	    IsIso (sheafification.map f) ↔
220	      (opensTopology X).W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj).map f) := by
221	  have e := _root_.PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms
222	      (J := opensTopology X) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
223	  constructor
224	  · intro h
225	    have h' : ((MorphismProperty.isomorphisms (SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)).inverseImage
226	        (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj))) f := h
227	    rw [← e] at h'
228	    exact h'
229	  · intro h
230	    have h' : (((opensTopology X).W).inverseImage
231	        (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj)) f := h
232	    rw [e] at h'
233	    exact h'
234	
235	/-- **The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism.**  The underlying
236	abelian-presheaf morphism of the module sheafification unit `η_P : P ⟶ P^#` is
237	*definitionally* the abelian sheafification unit `toSheafify J P.presheaf`
238	(`PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_unit_app`), which lies
239	in the weak-equivalence class `J.W` of the opens topology by
240	`GrothendieckTopology.W_toSheafify`.  Project-local: this is the `η_P ∈ J.W`
241	ingredient of the abelian-`J.W`-monoidality transfer underlying
242	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
243	lemma localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit (P : X.PresheafOfModules) :
244	    (opensTopology X).W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf X.ringCatSheaf.obj).map
245	      ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app P)) := by
246	  rw [PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_unit_app]
247	  exact (opensTopology X).W_toSheafify _
248	
249	/-- **Sheafification inverts the localization unit.**  `sheafification.map η_P` is an
250	isomorphism of sheaves of modules (the reflective-localization unit becomes invertible
251	after sheafifying).  Obtained by feeding `localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit` through the
252	localization criterion `isIso_sheafification_map_iff`.  Project-local: the `m = 0`
253	launching pad and the un-whiskered special case of
254	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
255	lemma isIso_sheafification_map_unit (P : X.PresheafOfModules) :
256	    IsIso (sheafification.map
257	      ((PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).unit.app P)) :=
258	  (isIso_sheafification_map_iff _).mpr (localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit P)
259	
260	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — relative tensor product as a coequalizer
261	
262	This section builds the **objectwise** content of `lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer`
263	(`relativeTensorCoequalizerIso`): over a commutative ring `S` and `S`-modules `M, N`,
264	the relative tensor product `M ⊗[S] N` is the coequalizer, *in the category of abelian
265	groups*, of the two `S`-action maps
266	
267	  `M ⊗[ℤ] S ⊗[ℤ] N  ⇉  M ⊗[ℤ] N`,    `m ⊗ s ⊗ n ↦ (s • m) ⊗ n`  /  `m ⊗ (s • n)`.
268	
269	This is the Mathlib-absent brick on which the strong-monoidality comparison
270	`isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit` rests: the underlying abelian presheaf of the
271	presheaf-level relative tensor `P ⊗_p Q` is, objectwise, exactly this coequalizer.  The
272	universal property is the abelian-group universal property of the relative tensor product,
273	packaged by `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`.  Everything here is axiom-clean.
274	
275	The promotion of this objectwise colimit to the presheaf category `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrp`
276	(where colimits are computed objectwise) and the identification of the whiskered unit
277	`η_P ▷ Q` with the induced map of coequalizers are the next steps; see the handoff note. -/
278	
279	namespace RelativeTensorCoequalizer
280	
281	open TensorProduct
282	
283	variable (S : Type u) [CommRing S] (M N : Type u)
284	  [AddCommGroup M] [Module S M] [AddCommGroup N] [Module S N]
285	
286	/-- The `S`-action map `S ⊗[ℤ] N → N`, `s ⊗ n ↦ s • n`, as a `ℤ`-linear map. -/
287	noncomputable def actN : (S ⊗[ℤ] N) →ₗ[ℤ] N :=
288	  TensorProduct.lift (LinearMap.mk₂ ℤ (fun s n => s • n)
289	    (fun s1 s2 n => add_smul s1 s2 n) (fun c s n => smul_assoc c s n)
290	    (fun s n1 n2 => smul_add s n1 n2) (fun c s n => smul_comm s c n))
291	
292	/-- The `S`-action map `M ⊗[ℤ] S → M`, `m ⊗ s ↦ s • m`, as a `ℤ`-linear map. -/
293	noncomputable def actM : (M ⊗[ℤ] S) →ₗ[ℤ] M :=
294	  TensorProduct.lift (LinearMap.mk₂ ℤ (fun m s => s • m)
295	    (fun m1 m2 s => smul_add s m1 m2) (fun c m s => smul_comm s c m)
296	    (fun m s1 s2 => add_smul s1 s2 m) (fun c m s => smul_assoc c s m))
297	
298	/-- Right action map `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) → M ⊗[ℤ] N`, `m ⊗ (s ⊗ n) ↦ m ⊗ (s • n)`. -/
299	noncomputable def actRmap : (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) →ₗ[ℤ] (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
300	  TensorProduct.map LinearMap.id (actN S N)
301	
302	/-- Left action map `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) → M ⊗[ℤ] N`, `m ⊗ (s ⊗ n) ↦ (s • m) ⊗ n`. -/
303	noncomputable def actLmap : (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) →ₗ[ℤ] (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
304	  (TensorProduct.map (actM S M) LinearMap.id).comp
305	    (TensorProduct.assoc ℤ M S N).symm.toLinearMap
306	
307	@[simp] lemma actRmap_tmul (m : M) (s : S) (n : N) :
308	    actRmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)) = m ⊗ₜ (s • n) := rfl
309	
310	@[simp] lemma actLmap_tmul (m : M) (s : S) (n : N) :
311	    actLmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)) = (s • m) ⊗ₜ n := rfl
312	
313	/-- The canonical projection `M ⊗[ℤ] N → M ⊗[S] N`, `m ⊗ n ↦ m ⊗ n`, as a `ℤ`-linear
314	map.  It is the cofork map exhibiting `M ⊗[S] N` as the coequalizer. -/
315	noncomputable def projL : (M ⊗[ℤ] N) →ₗ[ℤ] (M ⊗[S] N) :=
316	  (TensorProduct.liftAddHom
317	    { toFun := fun m =>
318	        (LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom (((TensorProduct.mk S M N) m).restrictScalars ℤ))
319	      map_zero' := by ext n; simp
320	      map_add' := fun m1 m2 => by ext n; simp }
321	    (fun r m n => by simp)).toIntLinearMap
322	
323	@[simp] lemma projL_tmul (m : M) (n : N) : projL S M N (m ⊗ₜ n) = m ⊗ₜ[S] n := rfl
324	
325	/-- The projection `M ⊗[ℤ] N → M ⊗[S] N` is surjective (it is the canonical
326	quotient map onto the relative tensor). -/
327	lemma projL_surjective : Function.Surjective (projL S M N) := by
328	  intro y
329	  induction y using TensorProduct.induction_on with
330	  | zero => exact ⟨0, map_zero _⟩
331	  | tmul m n => exact ⟨m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n, projL_tmul S M N m n⟩
332	  | add a b ha hb =>
333	    obtain ⟨pa, rfl⟩ := ha; obtain ⟨pb, rfl⟩ := hb; exact ⟨pa + pb, map_add _ _ _⟩
334	
335	/-- The two action maps become equal after the projection: this is the cofork
336	coequalizing condition, established at the level of `ℤ`-linear maps. -/
337	lemma projL_comp_act :
338	    (projL S M N).comp (actLmap S M N) = (projL S M N).comp (actRmap S M N) := by
339	  apply TensorProduct.ext'; intro m x
340	  induction x with
341	  | zero => rw [tmul_zero, map_zero, map_zero]
342	  | tmul s n =>
343	    change projL S M N (actLmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)))
344	      = projL S M N (actRmap S M N (m ⊗ₜ (s ⊗ₜ n)))
345	    rw [actLmap_tmul, actRmap_tmul, projL_tmul, projL_tmul, ← TensorProduct.smul_tmul',
346	      TensorProduct.tmul_smul]
347	  | add a b ha hb => rw [tmul_add, map_add, map_add, ha, hb]
348	
349	/-- Left action map as a morphism of abelian groups. -/
350	noncomputable def aL :
351	    AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
352	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (actLmap S M N).toAddMonoidHom
353	/-- Right action map as a morphism of abelian groups. -/
354	noncomputable def aR :
355	    AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N)) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] N) :=
356	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (actRmap S M N).toAddMonoidHom
357	/-- The projection as a morphism of abelian groups. -/
358	noncomputable def piMor :
359	    AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[ℤ] N) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of (M ⊗[S] N) :=
360	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (projL S M N).toAddMonoidHom
361	
362	@[simp] lemma piMor_apply (x) : (ConcreteCategory.hom (piMor S M N)) x = projL S M N x := rfl
363	
364	instance piMor_epi : Epi (piMor S M N) :=
365	  ConcreteCategory.epi_of_surjective (piMor S M N) (projL_surjective S M N)
366	
367	/-- The projection coequalizes the two action maps (as morphisms of abelian groups). -/
368	lemma coeq_condition : aL S M N ≫ piMor S M N = aR S M N ≫ piMor S M N := by
369	  ext x; exact LinearMap.congr_fun (projL_comp_act S M N) x
370	
371	/-- The cofork `M ⊗[ℤ] (S ⊗[ℤ] N) ⇉ M ⊗[ℤ] N → M ⊗[S] N` of abelian groups. -/
372	noncomputable def cofork : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N) :=
373	  Limits.Cofork.ofπ (piMor S M N) (coeq_condition S M N)
374	
375	/-- The descent map out of `M ⊗[S] N` induced by a cofork `s`: a pair of global
376	sections balanced under the `S`-action factors through the relative tensor.  This
377	is the universal property packaged by `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`. -/
378	noncomputable def descHom (s : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N)) :
379	    (M ⊗[S] N) →+ s.pt :=
380	  TensorProduct.liftAddHom
381	    { toFun := fun m =>
382	        { toFun := fun n => (ConcreteCategory.hom s.π) (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n)
383	          map_zero' := by rw [tmul_zero, map_zero]
384	          map_add' := fun n1 n2 => by rw [tmul_add, map_add] }
385	      map_zero' := by ext n; simp [zero_tmul]
386	      map_add' := fun m1 m2 => by ext n; simp [add_tmul] }
387	    (fun a m n => by
388	      simp only [AddMonoidHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk]
389	      have key :=
390	        congrArg (fun φ => (ConcreteCategory.hom φ) (m ⊗ₜ[ℤ] (a ⊗ₜ[ℤ] n))) s.condition
391	      simpa using key)
392	
393	@[simp] lemma descHom_tmul (s : Limits.Cofork (aL S M N) (aR S M N)) (m : M) (n : N) :

\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac}
  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
  \[
    a_L,\, a_R :
    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
    \;\rightrightarrows\;
    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
  \]
  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).

  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).

  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
  colimit cofork.

  The named members realise these steps in the category \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\):
  \(a_N, a_M\) are the action maps; \(a_L, a_R\) (\emph{actLmap}, \emph{actRmap})
  the two rows; \(\pi\) (\emph{projL}, surjective by \emph{projL\_surjective}) the
  cofork map; \emph{aL}, \emph{aR}, \emph{piMor} their packaging as abelian-group
  morphisms (\emph{piMor} an epimorphism, \emph{piMor\_epi}); \emph{coeq\_condition}
  (from the elementwise \emph{projL\_comp\_act}) the cofork condition;
  \emph{cofork} the cofork itself; \emph{descHom}/\emph{descMor} the descended map
  with factorisation \emph{descFac}; and \emph{isColimitCofork} the assembled
  universal property. The auxiliary \(*\_{\mathrm{tmul}}\)/\(*\_{\mathrm{apply}}\)
  equations are pure-\(\mathrm{rfl}\) computation rules on elementary tensors and
  are private implementation glue.
\end{proof}

  coequalizers assemble into a coequalizer in
  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\); naturality in \(U\) is the
  compatibility of the action maps with the restriction maps of
  \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\).
\end{proof}
